app.name: ChatGPT Agent (Operator)
app.bestFor: Consumer-facing agent that operates a real browser inside ChatGPT to complete multi-step web tasks end to end.
app.rankBasis: Placed at or near the top across all three cited 2026 browser-agent roundups and had first-party ChatGPT distribution; however, OpenAI's current help page now says the standalone ChatGPT Agent is no longer available and directs users to ChatGPT Work/cloud-browser workflows.
app.pricingPaid: No longer sold as a standalone product; its former paid access was bundled with ChatGPT plans (Plus included 40 agent requests/mo)
pros: deeply integrated into ChatGPT rather than requiring a separate automation stack
combined browsing, research and action in one guided workflow
asked for confirmation before consequential actions
cons: the standalone ChatGPT Agent is now officially unavailable
complex tasks could be slow and still require user intervention
logged-in browsing carries prompt-injection and sensitive-data risk

app.name: Claude for Chrome
app.bestFor: Anthropic's browser agent that drives Chrome to read pages and take actions on the user's behalf.
app.rankBasis: Featured as a top-tier browser agent in all three cited 2026 roundups; Anthropic has expanded it from research preview to a Chrome beta available across every paid Claude plan.
app.pricingPaid: Claude Pro $20/mo ($200/yr) - Claude in Chrome beta included with standard Pro usage capacity
features: read, click and navigate in Chrome
form filling and multi-step web actions
Claude Code and Cowork browser workflows
pros: works alongside the user's real logged-in Chrome session
strong fit for live debugging, UI verification and developer workflows
granular permission modes keep users in control
cons: paid-only and still labeled beta in the Chrome browser
prompt injection remains an inherent browser-agent risk
site visibility and extension reliability can still require troubleshooting

app.name: Perplexity Comet
app.bestFor: AI-native consumer browser that combines answering and agentic actions across tabs for research-heavy workflows.
app.rankBasis: Included by both cited 2026 agent-browser roundups and widely reviewed as the research-first AI browser; its free Chromium distribution, cited answers and cross-tab assistant give it broader consumer reach than most standalone agents.
app.pricingFree: Free Comet browser and Assistant with standard Perplexity limits
app.pricingPaid: Perplexity Pro $20/mo - extended Pro Search, advanced models and higher browser-agent limits
features: cited AI search in the browser
form filling, shopping and scheduling actions
pros: excellent research flow with answers and citations beside the page
familiar Chromium interface and extension support
useful cross-tab context without constant copy-paste
cons: agent actions and newer features can be inconsistent
desktop-focused experience has a learning curve
broad tab, history and account context raises privacy and prompt-injection concerns

app.name: Gemini in Chrome
app.bestFor: Google's built-in Chrome agent, generally available to Workspace users for in-browser assistance and actions.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited 2026 browser-agent roundups and is the only contender distributed natively through Chrome; its automation rank is tempered because multi-step Auto Browse is still a gradual US-only rollout for paid personal accounts.
app.pricingFree: Free Gemini in Chrome assistance where available; no multi-step Auto Browse
app.pricingPaid: Google AI Pro $19.99/mo - Auto Browse in Chrome (US only), 4x Gemini limits and 5 TB storage
features: Auto Browse for shopping, travel and reservations
pros: native Chrome surface with no separate browser to adopt
strong integration with Google Search and Workspace context
useful multi-tab understanding and voice interaction
cons: Auto Browse requires a paid personal plan and is US-only
Gemini in Chrome is still not available to every eligible user
sharing tabs and personal details with sites creates privacy risk

app.name: OpenAI Atlas
app.bestFor: OpenAI's standalone AI browser for agentic browsing, competing directly with Comet and Dia.
app.rankBasis: Named by both cited 2026 agent-browser roundups and backed by OpenAI's first-party browser distribution; it ranks below longer-established options because Agent Mode remains a paid preview and the browser is still comparatively new.
app.pricingFree: Free Atlas browser with Ask ChatGPT sidebar; Agent Mode not included
app.pricingPaid: ChatGPT Plus $20/mo - Agent Mode preview in Atlas with plan usage limits
features: optional browser memories and history context
Agent Mode for end-to-end web tasks
pros: the most direct browser experience for existing ChatGPT users
strong page, tab and browsing-context awareness
optional memories reduce repeated context setup
cons: Agent Mode requires a paid ChatGPT plan
newer browser with occasional reliability and compatibility issues
prompt injection remains a material risk for logged-in agent actions

skill.name: Browser Use
skill.bestFor: The recognized leader for AI browser agents (~107k★, ~89% WebVoyager): gives the LLM full control of the browser through an agent loop — the default for building custom browser agents.
skill.rankBasis: ~106.5k GitHub stars make it the category's most-adopted AI-native browser-agent framework; it is the autonomous-agent reference point in 2026 comparisons against Stagehand and Skyvern.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host); cloud free tier: 10 agent tasks/mo and 3 concurrent sessions
skill.pricingPaid: Browser Use Cloud Dev $29/mo - $29 monthly credits and 25 concurrent sessions
pros: largest community and ecosystem among AI-native browser frameworks
quick path from a natural-language goal to a working agent
flexible model and browser-provider support
cons: continuous LLM reasoning adds latency and token cost
less deterministic than code-first automation for repeated flows
self-hosting still requires browser, proxy and scaling infrastructure

skill.name: Playwright
skill.bestFor: Microsoft's foundational browser-automation framework (~93k★) — the deterministic substrate many AI agents (Stagehand, Skyvern) build on; the de-facto standard for scripted control and E2E testing.
skill.rankBasis: ~93.4k GitHub stars, Microsoft backing and an 8.7/10 TrustRadius score from 38 reviews make Playwright the most established deterministic browser-automation foundation in this track.
features: test runner, CLI and agent integrations
pros: reliable cross-browser API with strong auto-waiting
mature documentation, tooling and developer ecosystem
fast isolated contexts suit parallel test execution
cons: requires programming expertise and framework setup
selectors and test suites still need ongoing maintenance
full-browser execution is resource-heavy at large scale

skill.name: Stagehand
skill.bestFor: Playwright-based framework (~24k★) exposing structured act/extract/observe actions — deterministic where possible, AI only when needed.
skill.rankBasis: ~23.6k GitHub stars and repeated top-three placement in 2026 framework comparisons establish Stagehand as the leading hybrid alternative: deterministic Playwright/CDP code where possible, AI actions where needed.
pros: better control and repeatability than fully autonomous agents
caching can reduce model calls on stable repeated workflows
fits naturally into TypeScript browser-automation projects
cons: TypeScript-first approach is less convenient for Python teams
still requires an LLM and browser infrastructure in production
smaller ecosystem than Playwright or Browser Use

skill.name: Skyvern
skill.bestFor: Vision + LLM open-source agent (~23k★) resilient to layout changes; leads on form-filling (~85.85% WebVoyager).
skill.rankBasis: ~22.6k GitHub stars plus an independently cited ~85.8% WebVoyager result make Skyvern a top-four framework, especially for visual form-filling and workflows that must survive layout changes.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host); cloud Starter includes 1,000 credits/mo
skill.pricingPaid: Skyvern Cloud Hobby $29/mo - 30,000 credits/mo, faster execution and webhooks
pros: resilient on unfamiliar pages without hand-written selectors
particularly strong for forms and cross-site workflows
offers both developer SDKs and a no-code builder
cons: vision and model calls add latency and variable cost
self-hosting is heavier than a simple browser library
AGPL license is more restrictive for proprietary deployments

skill.name: Nanobrowser
skill.bestFor: Open-source Chrome extension (~13.5k★) for multi-agent web automation, running on your own LLM API keys as a free OpenAI-Operator alternative.
skill.rankBasis: ~13.5k GitHub stars make Nanobrowser the most-adopted open-source in-browser Operator alternative; 2026 reviews consistently highlight its local Chrome execution and bring-your-own-model flexibility.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source Chrome extension (bring your own LLM API key)
pros: no subscription and no vendor-controlled browser session
credentials remain in the user's local browser
flexible models let users balance quality and API cost
cons: LLM API usage is still paid separately
extension-based design is less suited to unattended server workloads
less production orchestration and observability than hosted platforms

skill.name: Steel Browser
skill.bestFor: Open-source browser API purpose-built for AI agents (~7.4k★): a self-hostable headless-browser backend for running web automations at scale.
skill.rankBasis: ~7.4k GitHub stars and more than one million browser hours served give Steel meaningful infrastructure adoption; it ranks as an agent-ready browser backend rather than a complete reasoning agent.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host); cloud Launch is $0/mo + usage with $30 one-time credits
skill.pricingPaid: Steel Cloud Scale $250/mo + usage - $100 monthly credits and production support
pros: purpose-built browser infrastructure for AI-agent workloads
self-hostable with a managed cloud upgrade path
strong session persistence, debugging and observability
cons: provides browser infrastructure, not the agent's reasoning loop
managed usage and proxy bandwidth can become expensive
self-hosting transfers fleet operations and anti-bot maintenance to the user

skill.name: Playwright Skill
skill.bestFor: A genuine SKILL.md browser-automation skill (~2.9k★): Claude autonomously writes and runs Playwright to test pages, fill forms and validate flows with screenshots. Ranked by adoption, not by being a skill.
skill.rankBasis: ~2.9k GitHub stars and 200+ forks make it one of the most-adopted genuine browser-automation Agent Skills; it ranks below the larger frameworks because it is a Claude-centric instruction layer over Playwright.
features: Claude Code plugin or standalone skill install
pros: turns natural-language QA tasks into inspectable Playwright code
loads on demand with a small persistent context footprint
generated scripts can be retained and rerun
cons: primarily designed around Claude Code's skill system
AI-generated tests still require review for assertions and edge cases
depends on a working Node, Playwright and browser environment

skill.name: Browserable
skill.bestFor: Smaller but notable open-source library and API (~1.2k★) for building AI browser agents and automations; self-hostable.
skill.rankBasis: ~1.2k GitHub stars and a claimed 90.4% WebVoyager score make Browserable a notable emerging self-hosted option, but its community and independent review footprint remain far smaller than the leaders above it.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source (self-host; bring your own LLM and remote-browser keys)
pros: simple self-hosted path with an npx setup flow
provider-neutral LLM and browser configuration
promising published WebVoyager performance
cons: small community and limited independent review evidence
requires separate LLM and remote-browser accounts for practical use
less mature documentation and production ecosystem than top frameworks

notes: Rich two-sided category. App track = consumer agentic browsers (ChatGPT Agent, Claude for Chrome, Comet, Gemini, Atlas). The skill track ranks the genuine SKILL.md skill AND the notable open-source browser-automation frameworks/repos TOGETHER by real adoption/reputation (GitHub stars), with kind ('skill'|'repo') as a tag only. This fixes the prior bug where Playwright Skill was placed #1 purely for being a skill: by real adoption Browser Use (~107k★, the recognized leader), Playwright (~93k★, the foundational framework) and Stagehand/Skyvern (~23k★) correctly outrank the ~2.9k★ Playwright Skill, which now sits at #7. Confidence tracks rank (high→medium→low). LaVague (~6k★) was excluded as unmaintained since early 2025. Benchmark figures (WebVoyager) and star counts come from cited sources / live GitHub. MCP servers (Playwright MCP) and managed-service-only offerings (Browserbase, Bright Data Agent Browser) remain out of scope for this track.
